sections.title: English future — a system of four choices
sections.content:
English has **no single future tense**. Instead it picks a form based on **how the speaker sees the situation**: was it decided before you spoke, or just now? Is there evidence in front of you? Is it a fixed schedule?

**Why it matters (roots-first)**: The future is a **system of choices** in the Verb Phrase root (#2 in the Tree Model). Czech encodes the future mainly with one form (*budu* + infinitive or perfective present), so Czech speakers often default to **will** for everything — and miss distinctions English speakers actually hear.

diagrams.title: The four futures — when to use each
rows: Decision now, prediction (no evidence), offer, promise
I'll get it. It'll probably rain. I'll help you.
Plan already decided; prediction from visible evidence
I'm going to study medicine. Look — it's going to rain.

sections.title: will — decision now, prediction, offer, promise
sections.content:
Use **will** when the future feeling comes *at the moment of speaking* — you hadn't decided before, or you're making a general guess with no present evidence.

**Often signalled by**: *I think, I hope, probably, maybe, I'm sure, I bet*.

items.good: The phone's ringing — I'll get it.
That bag looks heavy — I'll carry it for you.
I'll be there at 5. I won't tell anyone.

items.bad: Look at those clouds — it will rain.
items.good: Look at those clouds — it's going to rain.

items.bad: I will meet John tomorrow (already arranged).

sections.title: going to — plans and visible evidence
sections.content:
Use **going to** when the **intention existed before** you speak, or when the prediction is based on something **visible right now**.

**Rule of thumb**: *going to* points backwards (decided before) or to evidence in front of you. *will* points to the moment of speaking.

items.good: We're going to buy a new car next year.
Look at those clouds — it's going to rain.
He's running for the bus but it's leaving — he's going to miss it.
items.why: conclusion from what we can see

items.bad: The phone's ringing — I'm going to get it.

sections.title: Present continuous (arrangements) and present simple (timetables)
sections.content:
**Present continuous** — for **personal arrangements**: time + people + place are already locked in with someone else.
**Present simple** — for **public timetables**: you could read it off a board, screen, or printed schedule.

left.title: Present continuous — arrangement
points: She's flying to London on Friday.
We're having dinner with my parents on Sunday.
Someone else is involved; the slot is booked.

right.title: Present simple — timetable
points: Fixed by a programme, not by you.

items.why: personal plan — present continuous, not present simple

items.good: I'm starting an Italian course on Monday.
items.why: signed up — fixed arrangement → present continuous is more natural

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors
sections.content:
Three recurring mistakes — fixing them gives immediate, audible progress. The grammar is simple; **the choice is the work**.

**Next steps**: future continuous and future perfect (*I'll be working / I'll have finished*) are covered in [[topic:tenses_general|Tenses: General]].

items.bad: I will meet John tomorrow at 5. (already arranged)

items.why: personal plan → present continuous or going to

items.bad: Look at the sky — it will rain.
items.good: Look at the sky — it's going to rain.

questions.question: You already decided yesterday to see a film tonight. Which do you say?

options.a: I'm going to see a film tonight.
options.b: I will see a film tonight.

questions.question: The phone rings. You decide at that moment to answer it. Which do you say?
questions.explanation: Decision at the moment of speaking → will.

questions.question: You can see black clouds. You predict rain. Which is natural?
questions.explanation: Prediction based on present evidence (you can see the clouds) → going to.

questions.question: You want to offer to carry someone's bag. Which do you say?

options.b: I'm going to carry your bag.

questions.question: You have a general opinion about tomorrow's weather (no clouds now). Which is natural?

options.a: I think it's going to rain tomorrow.
options.c: I think it will rain tomorrow.
options.d: I think it will be raining tomorrow.

questions.explanation: General prediction (no present evidence), often with I think → will.

questions.question: I ____ (see) the dentist tomorrow at 10. (I already have an appointment.)
The phone's ringing. – I ____ (get) it.
questions.explanation: Decision at the moment of speaking → will.

questions.question: Look at those clouds – it ____ (rain).
questions.explanation: Prediction from present evidence → going to.

questions.question: I'm so tired. I ____ (have) a rest. (I just decided.)
She ____ (study) law at university. (She decided last year.)
That bag looks heavy. I ____ (carry) it for you.
questions.explanation: Offer: both will (I'll do it) and can (I'm able to) are natural here.

questions.question: We ____ (meet) at 6. (We arranged it yesterday.)
I think the match ____ (be) exciting.
He's running towards the bus but it's leaving – he ____ (miss) it.
questions.explanation: Prediction from what we can see now → going to.

questions.question: I promise I ____ (not tell) anyone.
They ____ (buy) a new car next year. (They've been saving for it.)
I don't think she ____ (come) to the party.

errorcorrection.title: Practice: Correct the form (will vs going to)

questions.question: Wrong: 'I will meet John at 5.' (You already arranged it.) Correct the sentence.
answers: I'm going to meet John at 5.
I am going to meet John at 5.
I'm going to meet John at 5
I am going to meet John at 5

questions.question: Wrong: (Phone rings) 'I'm going to get it.' (You decide at that moment.) Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'Look at the sky – it will rain.' (You can see the clouds.) Correct the sentence.
answers: Look at the sky – it's going to rain.
Look at the sky – it is going to rain.
Look at the sky – it's going to rain
Look at the sky – it is going to rain
questions.explanation: Prediction from present evidence → going to. Contractions (it's) and full form (it is) are both accepted.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I'm going to help you.' (You are offering now.) Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'I think it's going to be sunny tomorrow.' (No special evidence, just your guess.) Correct the sentence.
answers: I think it will be sunny tomorrow.
I think it'll be sunny tomorrow.
I think it will be sunny tomorrow
I think it'll be sunny tomorrow
questions.explanation: General prediction with I think → will. (Going to is also possible but will is more common for a simple guess.)

questions.question: Wrong: 'She will study in Prague.' (She decided and applied last month.) Correct the sentence.
answers: She's going to study in Prague.
She is going to study in Prague.
She's going to study in Prague
She is going to study in Prague

mc.title: Practice: Will and going to (multiple choice)
